nativeRange.habitat: The Platinum Hatchetfish inhabits slow-moving and still surface waters across the Amazon and Orinoco river basins. In the wild it frequents blackwater streams, flooded forest margins, and the sheltered edges of tributaries, typically where overhanging vegetation shades the water surface.

decorPreferences: tight-fitting lid (mandatory — extremely high escape risk)
floating plants (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce, hornwort) to diffuse surface light and provide cover
overhanging or emergent vegetation to mimic wild habitat
dark substrate to reduce reflective glare
driftwood and leaf litter for tannin release and natural aesthetics
open surface swimming lanes between plant cover

diet.notes: The Platinum Hatchetfish is a strict surface insectivore that, in the wild, feeds almost exclusively on insects and other invertebrates that fall or land on the water surface. In captivity it accepts small floating foods, including live or frozen fruit flies (Drosophila), wingless fruit flies, black mosquito larvae, small crickets, live or freeze-dried bloodworms, and surface-floating micro-pellets or mini insect sticks. The species will generally refuse food that has sunk, so surface presentation is essential. Regular supplementation with live or frozen fruit flies is strongly recommended for optimal conditioning. Frozen foods should be thawed before being offered at the surface.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (cardinal tetras, neon tetras, rummy-nose tetras)
dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma spp.) occupying lower tank zones
avoidWith: fast surface-feeding fish that compete for food
boisterous species that cause stress at the surface

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by demianlescano (2048x1152). Group of live Thoracocharax stellatus in a holding tank showing the diagnostic deep keel-shaped body and silvery-platinum sheen; multiple individuals represent its schooling nature; front fish sharp and well-lit.

Photo by Douglas Lopes (2048x1450). Crisp single specimen on black background, whole fish in frame, clearly showing the dramatically curved ventral keel, silvery body and spotted dorsal-fin marking. Sharpest diagnostic shot but a lone individual.

Aquarium group shot (1000x638) showing several hatchetfish and the in-tank hobbyist look with the silvery keel body; softer focus, kept only as a non-recommended extra for the planted-aquarium context.

summary: The Platinum Hatchetfish (Thoracocharax stellatus) is the largest hatchetfish regularly seen in the hobby, reaching 7–9 cm. Its deep, keel-shaped body and silvery sheen are unmistakable. A dedicated surface insectivore, it requires a tightly sealed tank and surface-presented live or frozen insect foods, and does best in schools of six or more in soft, slightly acidic water.
careNotes:
Thoracocharax stellatus is the largest of the commonly kept hatchetfishes, noticeably bigger than the Carnegiella species (black-winged hatchetfish, pygmy hatchetfish). Its deep, laterally compressed body with a dramatically curved ventral keel is adapted for surface life in South American river margins. The species is found throughout the Amazon and Orinoco basins and adjacent river systems, always in still or slow-moving waters near the surface.

The single most important husbandry requirement is a tight-fitting lid with no gaps — Thoracocharax stellatus is a powerful jumper capable of clearing considerable heights. Any gap in tank covers represents a lethal hazard. This escape risk is considered high and should be treated as non-negotiable.

Keep in groups of at least six individuals; smaller numbers tend to produce visible stress and timid behavior. A tank of 80 L (90 cm footprint minimum) provides adequate surface area for a small school. Soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–12) modelled on Amazonian blackwater conditions keeps them in best health. Floating plants such as Amazon frogbit or water lettuce are strongly recommended to provide surface cover and reduce the open-water anxiety that can trigger leaping. Subdued lighting and a dark substrate further reduce stress.

Diet must be surface-presented — the species will generally not chase or accept food that sinks even a short distance. Fruit flies (Drosophila melanogaster, wingless strains) are considered the gold-standard food and should be offered regularly alongside frozen or freeze-dried bloodworms, micro-pellets, and small floating insect-based sticks. Variety is important for long-term health and conditioning.

Avoid housing with boisterous or fin-nipping tankmates. The mid and lower tank zones are freely available to other peaceful community species. Avoid any fish large enough to predate the hatchetfish or fast enough to outcompete them at the surface for food. The smaller [black-winged-hatchetfish] and [pygmy-hatchetfish] — both Carnegiella relatives — can generally be co-housed with this species in a mixed hatchetfish surface school.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is considered difficult and rarely achieved outside of specialist setups. Spawning has been documented in very large, well-planted tanks with very soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5, conductivity <100 µS/cm) and a seasonal temperature drop as a trigger. Eggs are scattered among fine-leaved floating plants near the surface. Parents do not provide care and will consume eggs if not separated. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–36 hours at 26°C. Fry are tiny and require infusoria or commercially prepared first foods initially, graduating to micro-worms and baby brine shrimp. Commercial breeding is rare; most specimens in the trade are wild-caught.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Thoracocharax stellatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 8.5 cm), temperature range (23–28°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness, native range (Amazon and Orinoco basins and adjacent river systems), habitat, diet (surface insectivore, fruit flies, mosquito larvae), compatibility, tank requirements (tight lid, floating plants, schools of 6+), and breeding notes. Confirmed escape risk as high.

additional.name: FishBase — Thoracocharax stellatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max TL 9.0 cm per FishBase morphological maximum), native range (Amazon basin, Orinoco, and La Plata drainages), taxonomy (Gasteropelecidae, Characiformes), temperature (23–28°C). Confirmed wide South American distribution.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Hatchetfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade and care perspective. Confirmed surface-dwelling insectivore, tight lid requirement, soft acidic water preference, minimum school of 6, and floating plant recommendations. Noted Thoracocharax as the larger genus versus Carnegiella. Fruit fly supplementation recommended.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Hatchetfish Species Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary identification and care data. Described Thoracocharax stellatus as the largest commonly traded hatchetfish, confirmed silvery-platinum coloration, deep keel body form, Amazonian blackwater origin, and surface insectivore diet. Confirmed captive breeding is rare and difficult.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Hatchetfishes of South America
additional.notes: Historical and taxonomic reference. Contextualised Thoracocharax stellatus within the Gasteropelecidae family, noted its wide Amazonian distribution, escape-flight behaviour (using enlarged pectoral muscles for brief aerial gliding above the water surface), and specialist feeding on surface-fallen insects. Reinforced soft-water biotope requirements.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is considered difficult and is rarely achieved. When spawning has been documented, it has taken place in large, well-planted tanks with very soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5, conductivity below 100 µS/cm) and a gradual, seasonal drop in temperature as a trigger. Eggs are scattered among fine-leaved floating plants near the surface, and the parents offer no care — they should be separated from the eggs to prevent predation. Fry are very small at hatching and require infusoria or prepared first foods initially, graduating to micro-worms and baby brine shrimp. The majority of specimens available in the trade are wild-caught.
